title: Greetings and the respect register
summary: Why 'Ẹ' vs 'O' matters more than any other word you'll learn.
body:
In Yorùbá, the difference between respectful and familiar speech isn't a stylistic nicety — it's a moral signal. The pronoun **ẹ** (and verb forms that take it) marks respect: to elders, in-laws, strangers, customers, or anyone older than you. Using **o** (familiar) to someone who should get **ẹ** can read as aggressive or dismissive.

Rule of thumb for a 20-something:
- **Strangers, elders, shopkeepers older than you, official settings** → ẹ
- **Friends your age, close family your age or younger** → o

When in doubt, default to ẹ. Nobody is ever offended by being shown extra respect.

Greetings also shift by time of day:
- Morning: **Ẹ káàárọ̀**
- Afternoon: **Ẹ káàsán**
- Evening: **Ẹ kúurọ̀lẹ́**
- A generic, anytime greeting among peers: **Báwo ni**

Yorùbá greetings are acts, not small talk. Skipping the greeting when entering a room, shop, or conversation is genuinely rude — more so than in English.

title: Tones 101 — the three music notes of Yorùbá
summary: Every vowel carries a tone. Get this wrong and 'husband' becomes 'yam'.
body:
Yorùbá is tonal. Every syllable carries one of three pitches:

- **High** — marked with an acute accent: **á, é, í, ó, ú**
- **Mid** — unmarked: **a, e, i, o, u**
- **Low** — marked with a grave accent: **à, è, ì, ò, ù**

Same consonants and vowels, different tones, different meanings:

| Word | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Ọkọ** (mid-mid) | husband |
| **Ọ̀kọ̀** (low-low) | spear |
| **Ọkọ̀** (mid-low) | vehicle |

Subdots (ẹ, ọ, ṣ) are **not tones** — they change the vowel or consonant quality (open 'eh', open 'aw', 'sh'). You need both the subdot AND the tone mark on the same letter — that's why you see stacks like **ẹ́** or **ọ̀**.

Practical advice: don't try to reason about tones. Listen, copy, repeat. Your ear will rewire in weeks. The app's audio is your tool — play it before you read.

title: Ọ̀yọ́ vs Lagos — which Yorùbá are you learning?
summary: Quick primer on the major dialects.
body:
'Yorùbá' covers a dialect continuum across south-west Nigeria, Benin, and parts of Togo. The three major clusters:

- **Ọ̀yọ́** — the basis of Standard Yorùbá taught in schools. What you'll see in textbooks and on the news.
- **Lagos/Ẹ̀gbá** — urban, code-switches heavily with English and Nigerian Pidgin. What 20-somethings in Lagos actually speak day-to-day.
- **Ìjẹ̀bú, Èkìtì, Ìjẹ̀ṣà, Ondo** — regional variants; stronger tonal contrasts and some different vocabulary.

This app teaches **Standard Yorùbá** for the core deck (so you're understood everywhere) and **Lagos register** for the slang deck (so you sound like your friends, not a newsreader).
